claims.claim: On 18 May 2026 the Parliamentary Special Select Committee chaired by Azalina Othman Said wrapped its sixth meeting on the bill splitting the Attorney-General and Public Prosecutor.
claims.source: BusinessToday reported on 18 May 2026 that the draft was finalised and supported during the PSSC's sixth meeting; The Star and Bernama also reported the 18 May update.
claims.correction: No correction required, but use 'reported finalised after the sixth meeting' rather than implying the report text is public.
claims.stale_check: No later public PSSC report was located during this run.

claims.claim: The bill will be re-tabled on 22 June 2026.
claims.source: The Star and The Edge reported that the AG-PP separation bill is expected to be tabled during the parliamentary sitting beginning 22 June 2026.
claims.correction: Use 'expected to be tabled during the sitting beginning 22 June' unless the exact order paper is available.
claims.stale_check: No conflicting newer parliamentary order paper was found.

claims.claim: The 2018 Pakatan Harapan manifesto promised the AG-PP split immediately.
claims.source: Widely cited references to Buku Harapan Promise 15; primary manifesto PDF not retrieved in this run.
claims.correction: Retain only if the final dossier has the primary Buku Harapan PDF in its source bundle.

claims.claim: Article 145(1) puts AG appointment with the Yang di-Pertuan Agong on the Prime Minister's advice.
claims.stale_check: Constitutional text verified as currently available.

claims.claim: Article 145(3) vests prosecutorial discretion in the Attorney General and excludes Syariah court, native court and court-martial proceedings.
claims.stale_check: Constitutional text verified as currently available.

claims.claim: The bill splits the office, not the appointment route.
claims.source: The current AG route is PM advice under Article 145(1). The proposed PP route reported in the Malaysian Bar resolution is YDPA appointment in discretion, on JLSC recommendation and after consultation with the Conference of Rulers. Bernama reported the AG saying the bill makes no provision for the PM in PP appointment.
claims.correction: Replace with: 'The bill changes the formal appointment route, but critics argue executive influence remains indirectly present through the JLSC architecture and the AG's role on it.'
claims.stale_check: If the revised bill text differs from the February/March draft, this must be rechecked against the published bill.

claims.claim: The JLSC sits under Article 138; its chair is the Public Services Commission chair; the sitting AG or Solicitor General sits on the JLSC.
claims.correction: No correction required, but phrase precisely as 'the AG or, if the AG is an MP or appointed otherwise than from the Judicial and Legal Service, the Solicitor General'.
claims.stale_check: Constitutional text verified as currently available.

claims.claim: The JLSC chair is appointed by the Agong on the PM's advice.
claims.source: Article 139(4) says PSC members, including the chair, are appointed by the YDPA in his discretion after considering PM advice and after consultation with the Conference of Rulers. The analysis compresses this into 'on PM advice', which loses constitutional nuance.
claims.correction: Use: 'The JLSC chair is the PSC chair, and PSC members are appointed by the YDPA in his discretion after considering PM advice and after consultation with the Conference of Rulers.'
claims.stale_check: Constitutional text verified as currently available.

claims.claim: Ten government PKR MPs led by Rafizi Ramli demanded a parliamentary veto on the new prosecutor.
claims.source: The Edge reported on 24 February 2026 that 10 MPs led by Rafizi sought parliamentary confirmation and a Dewan Rakyat vote before the candidate's name was submitted to the YDPA.
claims.stale_check: No later reversal by the same MPs was found in this run.

claims.claim: None of the ten Rafizi-led PKR MPs were appointed to the PSSC.
claims.source: The 10 MPs are named by The Edge; BusinessToday states the PSSC comprised 11 MPs but does not list all members. Official parliamentary membership record was not retrieved in this run.
claims.correction: Verify against the official Parliament/PSSC record before publication. Until then, soften to: 'They were not obviously represented in the reported PSSC composition available to us.'
claims.stale_check: PSSC composition could change or be clarified by official records.

claims.claim: Six committee meetings to call it 'the best compromise'. Zero changes to who picks the prosecutor.
claims.source: The 'sixth meeting' is supported, but 'zero changes to who picks the prosecutor' is too strong because the proposed PP route differs formally from the AG's Article 145(1) PM-advice route.
claims.correction: Replace with: 'Six committee meetings to call it a compromise. The new route is formally different, but critics say the selection architecture still leaves executive influence insufficiently removed.'
claims.stale_check: Must be rechecked if the revised bill text is released.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: The bill splits the office, not the appointment route.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The proposed Public Prosecutor route is not the same as the current Attorney General route. The central critique is directionally arguable only if framed as indirect executive influence, not as 'unchanged appointment route'.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Federal Constitution Article 145(1), proposed Article 145A as reproduced in Malaysian Bar resolution, Bernama report quoting AG Dusuki.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Zero changes to who picks the prosecutor.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: This collapses a formal route change into a rhetorical conclusion. It should distinguish formal appointment mechanics from structural influence concerns.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Federal Constitution Article 145(1), Malaysian Bar resolution on proposed Article 145A, Bernama 23 February 2026.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: The 3 March select committee seated eleven MPs plus Azalina as chair. Not one of the ten.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The full official membership list was not retrieved in this run. One available report says the committee comprises 11 MPs; the exact count and exclusion claim need primary parliamentary verification.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: BusinessToday 18 May 2026 gives only the 11-MP description.

omitted_facts: Bernama reported on 23 February 2026 that AG Tan Sri Mohd Dusuki Mokhtar said the bill makes no provision for the Prime Minister in the appointment of the Public Prosecutor. This directly complicates the dossier's 'same route' thesis.
The Malaysian Bar's own resolution supports the structural concern, but it frames the issue as JLSC/AG influence and lack of parliamentary oversight, not as an unchanged formal route.

Article 139(4) is the more precise constitutional route for the PSC chair, and therefore indirectly for the JLSC chair; it uses the formula 'in his discretion but after considering the advice of the Prime Minister and after consultation with the Conference of Rulers'.
source_assessment: The source base is fairly broad: Constitution text, Malaysian Bar resolution, Bernama, The Star, The Edge, BusinessToday and opposition/backbencher reporting. The major weakness is that the revised PSSC report and revised bill text were not public in the sources located, and the official PSSC membership list was not retrieved. The dossier's legal sources are strong, but its central appointment-route inference is overstated.
overall_assessment: The dossier is publishable only after a material correction to the central thesis. The safe framing is not that the appointment route is unchanged, but that the formal route changes while critics argue independence remains incomplete because the JLSC architecture, AG/Solicitor General role and absence of parliamentary confirmation may preserve executive influence. Without that correction, the hook, reframe, analogy and view cards create a misleading impression.
